This next section, so we've gone over supplements, okay? We've gone over nutrition. Now what I want to do is go over the fitness aspect of this fulfillment. This is probably where there's going to be the most variety, because everybody has different styles. And so, I'm going to tell you what I think is best for level zero general population that is entirely deconditioned, okay? And I'm also going to preface this with saying that this is something that I think everybody should do, because chances are they're not doing it anyway, and I'll get into that exactly in a second. But there's going to be a progression in the fitness aspect of this, similar to the progression of how there was increases in expectations for nutrition, okay? So at week zero times zero, there's going to be no expectations of exercise of anybody, okay? What we are going to do though is have them track what their activity is. And so, in this stage of the game, it's, you don't really have to do anything, or pedometer, and use that to track your steps. So you're tracking your body weight daily, you're also going to be tracking your steps daily too, okay? And for the first two weeks, that is all that will be required of you. The day, just by virtue of this person now tracking their steps, when they didn't track their steps of tension and kids, yada, yada, yada. So bear with me here, very important, just want to get right off the gate. I don't think there has to be a specific exercise prescription, just that they're starting to track their activity, and for that reason, their activity will go up. Bingo, bingo, okay? So that is for weeks one and two. After you go through your first check-in, you look at that data they got from their steps, you know, you pull it out of their phone, whatever, and you'll be like, okay, you got some high days, you got some low days, some days you're more active than others, whatever. Perfect. Now, all this person is going to be required to do for the next step, is take the average of those steps, and make that their minimum steps per day. So you take the last 14 days of steps, obviously remove any ridiculous outliers, like if they didn't carry their pedometer with them or something for a day, this is your new step minimum. So if you take the average of 14 days, let's say there's three numbers that don't make sense, you kick those out. Now that average is 5,000, you say, all right, Sally, I need the minimum amount of steps that you take per day based off of this measuring apparatus to be 5,000, and that is the start of their prescriptive exercise, okay? And this is where we are in a dedicated, just get moving the first two weeks, and in the second two weeks, you stay moving, all right? You stay moving with them. Now, I don't really believe in the 10,000 calorie rule. That sounds amazing. The 10,000 steps, their minimum step count is 5,000. Then over the next four weeks, or I guess two weeks or three weeks, depending on where they are, you want to slowly increase the minimum amount of steps to be 8,000, trend it up. So if you set the minimum step to up to 5,000 and then up getting 5,500, great. Next week, go to 6,000. Week after that, go to 6,500. It's into infinity, which is like a human amount of activity, so it's all good. The 8,000 steps a day is a total human amount of activity. And when you get into the fat loss and performance phase, which is starting at week eight or your fifth check-in, this is when you start to incorporate more specific activity into this person's lifestyle, okay? And also where you start to become more specific with their supplement needs if required. Obviously, it's not going to hurt anyone to stay on the ultimate package the entire time. However, I understand for some people, it might not be appropriate for them, and this is where you want to gear their supplementation towards fat loss or performance, depending on the person's goals. But the caloric load is still such that they're losing fat. All right, moving on. Basically, you expecting them to walk every day. That's it. Moving on to the performance phase, we want to have a minimum of two training sessions per week. Is dedicating three hours out of their week, two 90-minute sessions or two hour-long sessions, as dedicated time to train? And if they're training at home or at your gym or whatever, just have it be something. And if they're training at home, they go with calisthenics, go with rubber bands, go with medicine balls, just any sort of dedicated movement for two or three hours each week divided into two sessions. That is the only expectation. Going on to week 10, the next two weeks, you're still expecting them to have two training sessions per week. We'll move up to four, by the way. Two training sessions per week, but having them increase the intensity of the training sessions they have. Sort of thing if they're capable. So you're still at two sessions per week, but you're increasing the intensity. Two weeks later, all right, now we're at week 12. At this point, they've been exposed to new methods of exercise, and you increase their exercise variety based on whatever method of exercise they are currently leaning towards. So at this point, the person has figured out the sort of exercise that they like to participate in, all right? And they're at 50% increase in activity, structured activity, but they're still maintaining their step count. And the reality is that if they're very out of shape, then training any one of these things will improve the other two if only by accident. So if a person would rather increase their endurance, then you can maybe start some sort of like couch to fight. I'll let, and then if you have a person that wants to increase some mobility, maybe you have them start doing some sort of Pilates or yoga, whatever sort of program you want them to incorporate. Okay, now they're training at least three times a week for an hour to 90 minutes. All right, so we're in between three and four and a half hours of dedicated activity per week, now heading towards a specific fitness goal of endurance, strength, or mobility. Okay, so that is very straightforward. And the next meeting you have at week 16, okay, I should back up a little bit and say once you have the goal that they have, you're going to want to spend a little time to create a program for them or find one and then give it to them. At week 16, you present the dedicated training plan to meet their new fitness goal. And from week 16 on, that's where the recovery phase starts. And this is what's so great because now that you're increasing the amount of specific and dedicated activity they have at this point in the game, you're also systematically increasing their calories. So at week six at four sessions per week. And now at weeks 20 to infinity, the expert by the door, they have found that is dedicated towards a fitness goal of their choosing. And this is very important to me because increasing a fitness goal increases a skill set they have. It increases their adherence to the program. And it's also fun. I want to iterate that exercise is for wellness. It is not for weight loss. And so if you have huge amounts of activity throughout, really it ends up creating a poor relationship with exercise and that they're afraid to either they're either afraid to stop or they fall off and never want to do it again. So by focusing all of the weight loss and leaning that on the nutrition side, when you start to incorporate exercise, yes, you burn more calories. But the whole point is that you develop a skill, you have fun and they reach a fitness goal. Okay. And women in particular fat off, but it is also the component that changes the shape of your body. If you just lose fat through nutrition. Yes, you will lose fat, your body will stay the same shape. It is only after incorporating exercise in a specific fashion that your body will change shape, even if you stay the same way. And that's why I like the incorporation of specific exercise during the start of the recovery phase. So you can increase calories, but also fun. Thank you.
